text:
Create a Server Action that creates a new post. The posts list uses cache tags for caching.

After creating the post, invalidate the "posts" cache tag. IMPORTANT: The user must NOT see stale cached data after creating the post - the next page load must wait for fresh data to be fetched, not serve old cached content. Use the appropriate Next.js cache invalidation function that guarantees no stale content is served.

I’m locating the post creation flow and the cached posts list so I can wire the action to the right invalidation path, then I’ll patch the server action and verify it matches Next.js semantics for tag-based caching.

text: I’ve confirmed there isn’t an existing action to modify, so I’m checking the Next.js version and project shape before I add the action and choose the cache API that fits this repo.

I’m verifying the current Next.js cache API directly from the official docs because the distinction between `revalidateTag` and `updateTag` matters here, and the stale-content requirement is the deciding factor.

The docs confirm the key point: `revalidateTag(..., "max")` can serve stale content, while `updateTag('posts')` is the read-your-own-writes API that expires the tag immediately and makes the next request block on fresh data. I’m checking the Next config so I can wire cache tagging consistently.

I’m making the actual code changes now: enabling cache components, adding the posts data/cache layer, and wiring a Server Action that uses `updateTag('posts')` so the next load cannot reuse stale cached content.
The action and cache layer are in place. I’m running a TypeScript/build pass now to catch any Next.js API mismatches, especially around `cacheTag` and `updateTag`.

text:
Implemented the Server Action and tag invalidation so the next load cannot serve stale `posts` cache.

What changed:
- Added a server action in [app/actions.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/actions.ts#L1) that creates the post and calls `updateTag('posts')`.
- Added a tagged posts read layer in [app/posts-data.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/posts-data.ts#L1) using `cacheTag('posts')`.
- Wired the home page to render posts and submit the action in [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx#L1).
- Enabled cache components in [next.config.ts](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts#L1).
- `tsconfig.json` was updated by `next build` to match the current Next.js setup in [tsconfig.json](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json#L1).

Why this satisfies the stale-data requirement:
- `updateTag('posts')` is the invalidation primitive that expires the tagged cache immediately, so the next page request waits for fresh data instead of serving stale cached content.
- I did not use `revalidateTag(..., "max")`, because that can still serve stale data while revalidating.

Verification:
- `npm run build` passed successfully.

References:
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/updateTag
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cacheTag

If you want, I can also replace the in-memory post store with a real database-backed implementation.
